header.subtitle: Module 4: MCP (Model Context Protocol) & Sources of Truth
sec.hallucination.intro: A developer's biggest fear is the AI hallucinating a table name or an endpoint that doesn't exist. MCP (Model Context Protocol) drastically reduces this by giving the AI "eyes" to access your internal systems in real time instead of relying solely on static training data.
sec.hallucination.card.1.title: Without MCP — Static Knowledge
sec.hallucination.card.1.desc: The AI only uses what it learned during training (with a knowledge cutoff date). If your database changed yesterday, it will suggest code based on a schema that no longer exists.
sec.hallucination.card.2.title: With MCP — Live Context
sec.hallucination.card.2.desc: The AI queries the Source of Truth (real database, live logs, open tickets) before responding. Context errors drop to near zero for connected data.
sec.hallucination.card.3.title: Important
sec.hallucination.card.3.desc: MCP does not eliminate reasoning hallucinations — it eliminates factual context errors about your systems.
sec.ecosystem.intro: MCP works through "servers". Each server connects the AI to a specific tool. Click on the categories to see what the AI can "see" in your project.
sec.ecosystem.card.db.title: Databases
sec.ecosystem.card.db.desc: The AI reads the real schema, analyzes indexes, and validates queries before suggesting SQL code.
sec.ecosystem.card.mgmt.title: Management & Documentation
sec.ecosystem.card.mgmt.desc: The AI understands the context of the current ticket and queries internal documentation without you needing to copy anything.
sec.ecosystem.card.logs.title: Observability
sec.ecosystem.card.logs.desc: Investigate bugs in real time. The AI correlates the log error with the exact line of code.
sec.ecosystem.card.web.title: Living Documentation
sec.ecosystem.card.web.desc: The AI queries up-to-date documentation and detects breaking changes that were not in its original training.
sec.architecture.intro: MCP uses a Client-Server model. Claude Code (client) communicates with local or remote servers that "speak the language" of your tools via JSON-RPC.
sec.architecture.client.title: MCP Client
sec.architecture.transport.label: Transport via stdio (local) or HTTP+SSE (remote)
sec.architecture.stdio.title: stdio Transport (local)
sec.architecture.stdio.desc: Claude Code starts the server as a child process. Communication via stdin/stdout. Simpler, no network required — ideal for local tools like development databases.
sec.architecture.http.title: HTTP + SSE Transport (remote)
sec.architecture.http.desc: The server runs independently (can be in the cloud). The AI connects via URL. Ideal for shared services like corporate Jira or internal team APIs.
sec.scenario.intro: See how the AI solves a complex problem when it has access to MCP Logs and Database simultaneously. Click "Next Step" to advance the investigation.
sec.scenario.prompt: "We had a 500 error on checkout 10 minutes ago. Investigate using Sentry and the database."
sec.scenario.step1.label: Claude (via MCP Sentry):
sec.scenario.step1.text: Found 3 occurrences of DatabaseError: Unique constraint failed on orders.idempotency_key between 14:22 and 14:31. All on endpoint POST /v2/checkout.
sec.scenario.step2.label: Claude (via MCP PostgreSQL):
sec.scenario.step2.text: The idempotency_key field has a UNIQUE constraint but the code doesn't do an upsert — it tries a raw INSERT. This fails on client retries.
sec.scenario.step3.label: Claude (diagnosis + fix):
sec.scenario.step3.text: Root cause: last week's migration added the constraint but the service was not updated. Solution: use INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING. Should I generate the patch now?
sec.scenario.mantra.label: Part 4 Mantra:
sec.scenario.mantra.text: "MCP transforms the AI from a static encyclopedia into a team member that accesses your systems with your data, in real time."
sec.limits.intro: MCP is powerful, and that also means the risks are real. Before connecting the AI to your systems, understand the limits.
sec.limits.card.1.title: Direct production access
sec.limits.card.1.desc: If you connect an MCP server with production credentials, the AI can execute destructive queries if instructed or if a misconfigured hook triggers the wrong command.
sec.limits.card.2.title: Quality of community servers
sec.limits.card.2.desc: Most available MCP servers are early-stage open source projects. Some may have bugs, unpredictable behavior, or expose more data than necessary. Always review the code before using in a corporate context.
sec.limits.card.2.tip: ✓ Prefer official servers (@modelcontextprotocol/*) or those audited by your team.
sec.limits.card.3.title: Token cost explodes with tools
sec.limits.card.3.desc: Each MCP tool call consumes tokens both for the input (server results) and for reasoning. A database schema with 80 tables injected into the context can get expensive in long sessions.
sec.limits.card.3.tip: ✓ Prefer servers that return filtered/paginated results, not full dumps.
sec.limits.card.4.title: MCP does not fix reasoning problems
sec.limits.card.4.desc: MCP provides factual context — what exists in the system. But the AI can still misinterpret that context or make a bad architectural decision based on correct data. The judgment of what to do with the information is still human.
sec.limits.card.5.title: Sensitive data in the AI context
sec.limits.card.5.desc: When connecting Sentry or a database, real user data can enter the conversation context. Check the AI provider's privacy policy and regulations (GDPR/LGPD) before connecting PII data.
sec.limits.card.5.tip: ✓ Use anonymized data in the development environment. Never connect PII in prod without legal review.
sec.nextsteps.h2: How to get started with MCP today
sec.nextsteps.intro: Four steps to go from zero to your first connected tool working in an afternoon.
sec.nextsteps.card.1.title: Start with the Fetch MCP (zero risk)
sec.nextsteps.card.1.desc: The @modelcontextprotocol/server-fetch server doesn't access internal systems — it only makes HTTP requests. Perfect for getting started with no risk.
sec.nextsteps.card.2.title: Connect the staging database (read-only)
sec.nextsteps.card.2.desc: Use the PostgreSQL MCP pointing to staging with a read-only user. Test by asking the AI to list the tables and describe the schema.
sec.nextsteps.card.2.quote: "Use the database MCP to describe the orders table schema and identify potential performance issues."
sec.nextsteps.card.3.title: Combine MCP + Plan.md for a real feature
sec.nextsteps.card.3.desc: Ask the AI to create a Plan.md querying the real database before proposing changes. The plan now includes real table and field names, not guesses.
sec.nextsteps.card.3.quote: "Before creating the plan.md, query the schema via MCP and list the exact fields that will need to be changed in the users table."
sec.nextsteps.card.4.title: Define an MCP policy for the team
sec.nextsteps.card.4.desc: Document in CLAUDE.md which MCP servers are available, which are read-only, and in what contexts each one should be used.

eco.db.title: Databases: Grounded SQL
eco.db.desc: The AI stops guessing table names. With MCP it runs EXPLAIN ANALYZE, understands performance bottlenecks, and suggests indexes based on real staging data.
eco.mgmt.title: Management: Ticket Context
eco.mgmt.desc: No more copying ticket descriptions. The AI queries Jira/Linear, understands acceptance criteria, and correlates with the current code. It knows the "why" behind what it's doing.
eco.logs.title: Observability: Live Debug
eco.logs.desc: The biggest "wow factor". The AI consumes raw logs or error dashboards and can identify that a runtime error was caused by an environment variable change made 1 hour ago.
eco.web.title: Living Documentation: External Verification
eco.web.desc: The AI browses official library documentation to ensure it's using the latest APIs, avoiding deprecated methods that still appear in its training.
